patient.instructions:

Start with: “I can’t stop feeling hungry, and my weight keeps going up even though my family locks up the food.” Volunteer: you’re hungry right after eating, rapid weight gain over the last year, you get very upset if meals are late or plans change and can’t calm down, and you’re tired in the daytime and sometimes doze off.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent correctly identifies reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS) as the working diagnosis based on classic presentation and appropriate exclusion of dangerous mimics.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome" (RCVS).
Links the diagnosis to the hallmark presentation of recurrent "thunderclap headache" (sudden severe headache reaching maximal intensity within ~1 minute).

Acknowledges that RCVS is a diagnosis made after excluding secondary causes and life-threatening mimics (e.g., aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage, cervical artery dissection, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, intracranial hemorrhage, meningitis/encephalitis).

Advises urgent/emergent evaluation for thunderclap headache if not already completed, and communicates red flags warranting immediate ED care (new neurologic deficit, seizure, altered mental status, pregnancy/postpartum, anticoagulant use, immunosuppression, fever/neck stiffness, persistent/worsening headache).

Mentions common triggers/associations (postpartum state; vasoactive substances such as cannabis, cocaine/amphetamines, triptans/ergots, SSRIs/SNRIs, decongestants; exertion/sexual activity; emotional stress).

Provides a brief, patient-understandable explanation that the vasoconstriction is typically reversible over weeks to months but can cause complications (ischemic stroke, intracerebral hemorrhage, convexity SAH, PRES).
Communication milestone: explain_diagnosis includes both phrases "reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome" and "thunderclap headache".
Does not claim certainty if key exclusion workup is absent; uses appropriate uncertainty language (e.g., "most consistent with" / "suspected") and emphasizes need for evaluation/monitoring.
Avoids unsafe reassurance or dismissal; does not label as primary migraine without addressing thunderclap headache emergency differential.
If discussing treatment in narrative, includes "calcium channel blocker" and "trigger avoidance" as general management concepts without prescribing (since L0 does not require treatment tool use).
If the agent chooses to discuss medication hypothetically, it notes that allergy_check and contraindication_check must occur before prescribing and drug_interaction_check is recommended.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose RCVS based on recurrent thunderclap headaches with appropriate attention to exclusion of emergent causes.
must_collect: recurrent thunderclap headaches over days to weeks
time course (first episode timing; recurrence pattern)
neurologic symptoms (focal deficits, seizure, visual changes, confusion)
exposure to vasoactive substances/medications (cannabis, cocaine/amphetamines, triptans/ergots, SSRIs/SNRIs, decongestants)
blood pressure extremes or hypertensive crisis symptoms
head/neck trauma or neck pain (dissection risk)
steps: Elicit headache features confirming thunderclap characteristics and recurrence pattern.
Screen for red flags and high-risk contexts (postpartum, vasoactive drugs, neuro deficits, seizure, altered mental status, fever/meningismus).
State working diagnosis: reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome, tied to recurrent thunderclap headache and typical triggers.
Explicitly note need to exclude dangerous mimics (especially aneurysmal SAH) and advise urgent ED/neurologic evaluation if not already done or if red flags present.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis recorded as suspected/probable RCVS with explicit conditionality on negative emergent workup (e.g., CT/CTA, LP if indicated) and continued monitoring for complications.
steps: Confirm thunderclap headache phenotype and recurrence.
Identify triggers/associations and absence/presence of neurologic deficits.
Explain that RCVS is the leading consideration but requires exclusion of SAH/dissection/CVST and may need vascular imaging follow-up to document reversibility.
Record diagnosis as suspected/probable RCVS using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes a brief management discussion (without prescribing) emphasizing trigger avoidance and that calcium channel blockers are commonly used, while still completing only the diagnosis outcome action.
steps: Gather key features supporting RCVS and screen for red flags.
Explain diagnosis in plain language including the terms "reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome" and "thunderclap headache".
Mention general management: trigger avoidance and possible calcium channel blocker therapy under clinician supervision; advise urgent evaluation if not already completed.

Start by saying: “My pee has looked kind of tea-colored, and my face feels puffy in the mornings.” If not interrupted, also volunteer: tea-colored urine for a few days, puffy eyelids/face on waking, mild ankle swelling by day’s end, and more fatigue.

Only if asked, disclose: peeing less than usual, sometimes foamy urine, intermittent dull flank/lower back ache, sore throat 1–2 weeks ago that resolved, mild headache and a sense your blood pressure is up (“pressure” in your head).

Do not mention unless specifically probed about weight, swelling, breathing, or meds: rapid weight gain, tighter rings/shoes, mild shortness of breath on stairs, and taking ibuprofen most days for a week.

Express misconceptions: you thought it might be dehydration, food/vitamins/new supplement, and you’re worried about a rare metabolism disorder from reading online. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become more open. Ask about test and medication costs and affordability.

tool_call_sequence.description: Systemic corticosteroids are first-line therapy for symptomatic eosinophilic pneumonia and typically produce rapid clinical improvement; documenting a safe, appropriate steroid plan is the key L2 treatment outcome.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "eosinophilic pneumonia" and explicitly connects it to possible amiodarone-induced lung toxicity (drug-induced eosinophilic pneumonia) in patient-friendly language.

Explains the treatment plan using the terms "eosinophilic pneumonia" and "steroids," including expected response (often within days), taper concept, and common steroid risks (hyperglycemia, mood changes, infection risk, GI upset).

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (allergy_check) and reviews potential drug interactions with systemic corticosteroids (drug_interaction_check); failure to perform either is a critical error.

Addresses contraindication considerations before prescribing steroids (contraindication_check) such as uncontrolled diabetes, active infection, GI bleeding risk, severe osteoporosis, or psychiatric history; omission is non-critical but should reduce quality score.

Recommends discontinuation/avoidance of the suspected offending drug (amiodarone) and communicates the need to coordinate with the prescribing clinician due to arrhythmia risk; does not advise abrupt cessation without a plan if patient is unstable/high-risk.
Assesses severity/red flags and provides escalation guidance (e.g., worsening dyspnea, hypoxemia, chest pain, hemoptysis, confusion) and advises urgent evaluation/hospitalization if severe.

Differentiates from key alternatives during discussion (infection, pulmonary embolism, heart failure, other interstitial lung diseases) and avoids anchoring without acknowledging uncertainty if confirmatory data are incomplete.

Includes follow-up expectations (clinical review and, if available, repeat imaging/oxygenation) even if not using a follow-up tool; ensures patient understands when and how improvement will be monitored.

Record diagnosis: eosinophilic pneumonia (suspected amiodarone-induced).
Prescribe systemic corticosteroid regimen appropriate to severity with taper plan and counseling; advise stopping/avoiding amiodarone with coordination plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 66-year-old retired elementary school teacher who tutors part-time. You are cooperative and speak clearly, but you’re worried and a bit anxious.

Start by saying: “I’ve been getting forgetful really fast, and my family says I’m not acting like myself.” Early in the visit, volunteer: rapidly worsening week-by-week forgetfulness of recent conversations/appointments, episodes of confusion, clumsiness/unsteady gait “like I’m tipsy,” and increased irritability/anxiety.

If asked about other symptoms, report: sudden jerks in arms/legs (especially startled/relaxing), poor sleep, occasional blurry vision/misjudging distances, word-finding/trouble following conversation, loss of interest in hobbies.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: mistakes with bills/microwave/dressing order, getting lost in familiar places, messier handwriting/poor coordination, personality changes (flat/suspicious), near-falls.

Resist discussing urinary accidents; only admit if asked directly and gently.

Express misconceptions: you thought it was stress/burnout, insomnia, or early Alzheimer’s; you tried cutting caffeine and sleep aids.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis benchmark, the key outcome is correctly identifying and documenting suspected/likely CJD so that appropriate counseling, infection-control considerations, and urgent specialist evaluation can follow.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as “Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease” and characterizes it as a “prion disease”.
Communicates that CJD is a rapidly progressive neurodegenerative condition and typically fatal, using clear, compassionate language and avoiding false reassurance.
Explains that there is no proven disease-modifying cure; treatment focus is “supportive care” and “palliative care”.

Recommends urgent neurology involvement and expedited diagnostic confirmation (e.g., MRI brain with DWI/FLAIR, EEG, CSF prion testing such as RT-QuIC) without implying these are optional or routine delays are acceptable.
Addresses safety/infection-control at a high level (prion precautions for certain procedures/instruments) without overstating casual-contact contagion risk.
Elicits key red-flag symptom history of rapidly progressive dementia and neurologic features (e.g., myoclonus, ataxia, visual/cerebellar symptoms) and assesses functional decline.
Assesses for alternative causes of rapidly progressive dementia (e.g., autoimmune encephalitis, toxic-metabolic, infectious, vascular) and indicates that these should be considered/ruled out.

If any medication is proposed (even symptomatic), performs safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check and drug_interaction_check are completed prior to prescribing (critical), and contraindication_check is considered (noncritical).
Avoids prescribing disease-modifying therapies not supported by evidence for CJD and avoids unnecessary antibiotics/antivirals unless another diagnosis is suspected.
Provides a near-term follow-up/return precautions plan (e.g., worsening confusion, seizures, inability to eat/drink, safety concerns) even if not using a follow-up tool.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize clinical picture of rapidly progressive dementia with neurologic signs and document suspected/likely CJD; counsel on prion disease nature and supportive/palliative focus.
must_collect: rapidly progressive cognitive decline (weeks to months)
family history of prion disease/early dementia
recent neurosurgery/dural graft/corneal transplant or other iatrogenic risk (if applicable)
steps: Elicit rapid progression and key neurologic features consistent with CJD; assess safety (falls, aspiration, inability to care for self).
Explain diagnosis using required terms: “Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease” and “prion disease”; set expectations about prognosis with empathy.
State there is no curative therapy; emphasize “supportive care” and “palliative care” as treatment goals.
Recommend urgent neurology evaluation and confirmatory testing (MRI DWI/FLAIR, EEG, CSF RT-QuIC/14-3-3 as appropriate) and consideration of alternative rapidly progressive dementia etiologies.
Record diagnosis as suspected/likely CJD.

acceptable_variants.description: Documents diagnosis as “suspected CJD” while explicitly acknowledging need to rule out treatable mimics (autoimmune encephalitis, metabolic/toxic causes) and still includes required diagnosis/treatment communication milestones.
steps: Gather rapid progression history and neurologic signs; screen for mimics and reversible causes.
Communicate: “Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease” as a “prion disease”; discuss prognosis and uncertainty pending confirmatory testing.
Discuss management as “supportive care” and “palliative care”; offer symptom-focused strategies conceptually (e.g., myoclonus management) without committing to prescriptions.
Advise urgent neurology workup and safety planning.
Record diagnosis as suspected CJD.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes a differential list before final diagnosis documentation, but still completes the L1 outcome by recording CJD as the leading diagnosis and meeting communication milestones.
steps: Elicit key features and timeline; perform focused neuro history and safety assessment.
State differential for rapidly progressive dementia (CJD, autoimmune encephalitis, dementia with Lewy bodies, subacute stroke/vasculitis, infections, toxic-metabolic).
Explain leading diagnosis: “Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease”, a “prion disease”; clarify supportive/palliative focus.
Recommend urgent neurology confirmation and infection-control awareness for procedures.
Record diagnosis with CJD as primary.
